quiz_questions.question_text: What does Newton's second law state?

options.text: For every action force there exists an equal and opposite reaction force, acting on a different object as described by Newton's third law of motion
An object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity unless acted on by an unbalanced external net force
The net force on an object equals the product of its mass and its velocity, directed along the line of motion of the object at every instant in time
The acceleration of an object equals the net force acting on it divided by its mass (a = F_net/m)

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's second law: F_net = ma, or equivalently a = F_net/m. The acceleration is in the same direction as the net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the acceleration of a 5 kg object with a net force of 20 N acting on it?

options.text: 25 m/s²
100 m/s²
4 m/s²

quiz_questions.question_text: What net force is needed to accelerate a 10 kg object at 3 m/s²?
If the same force is applied to two objects with different masses, which one accelerates more?

options.text: Neither accelerates
The object with less mass
The object with more mass
Both accelerate equally

quiz_questions.explanation: a = F/m. For the same F, smaller m produces larger a. Less mass → less inertia → more acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you double the net force on an object while keeping the mass constant, what happens to the acceleration?

options.text: It quadruples
It doubles
It stays the same
It halves

quiz_questions.explanation: a = F/m. If F doubles and m is constant, then a doubles. Acceleration is directly proportional to net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you double the mass of an object while keeping the net force constant, what happens to the acceleration?

options.text: It halves
It stays the same
It quadruples
It doubles

quiz_questions.explanation: a = F/m. If m doubles and F is constant, then a halves. Acceleration is inversely proportional to mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the weight of a 10 kg object on Earth (g = 9.8 m/s²)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Weight = mg = 10 × 9.8 = 98 N. Weight is the gravitational force on an object.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is weight a force?

options.text: No — weight and force are completely different concepts
Yes — weight is the gravitational force acting on an object
Only in space
Only in metric units

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight = mg is the force of gravity on an object. It is measured in newtons and is a vector pointing toward the center of the Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1 kg object and a 2 kg object both have the same acceleration. Which has a greater net force acting on it?

options.text: Both objects have the same net force acting on them, because identical accelerations always require identical forces regardless of the masses involved
Neither object has a net force because only velocity matters for determining force, and acceleration is irrelevant to the relationship between force and mass
The 1 kg object has the greater net force because lighter objects always need more force to achieve any acceleration, as their low inertia demands more input
The 2 kg object — F = ma, so twice the mass with the same acceleration needs twice the force

quiz_questions.explanation: F = ma. If a is the same, then F is proportional to m. The 2 kg object requires F = 2a, while the 1 kg requires F = a.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the SI units in the equation F = ma?

options.text: F in pounds, m in grams, a in m/s
F in watts, m in kg, a in m/s²
F in joules, m in kg, a in m/s²
F in newtons (N), m in kilograms (kg), a in m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: In SI: 1 N = 1 kg·m/s². The equation F = ma requires these consistent units.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes Newton's second law?

options.text: The law stating that the net force on an object equals the product of its mass and acceleration (F_net = ma), with the acceleration directed along the net force
The law describing action-reaction pairs, which states that every force is accompanied by an equal and opposite force acting on a different object in the system
The law stating that objects resist changes in their state of motion due to inertia, remaining at rest or moving at constant velocity when no net force acts
The law of universal gravitation, which states that every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses over distance squared

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's second law quantifies the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration. It is the most fundamental equation of classical mechanics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes weight?

options.text: Exactly the same as mass in all situations and locations, since weight and mass are interchangeable terms that always refer to the same physical quantity
The gravitational force acting on an object, equal to mass times the local gravitational field strength (W = mg)
The measure of how tightly packed matter is within an object, calculated by dividing the total mass of the object by its volume in cubic meters
The perpendicular support force that a surface exerts on any object resting on it, acting upward to prevent the object from passing through the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight is a force vector directed downward (toward Earth's center). It equals mg and varies with location (different g), unlike mass which is constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes mass in Newton's second law?

options.text: The total volume of space that an object occupies, measured in cubic meters and determined by the physical dimensions and shape of the object in question
A type of force that is measured in kilograms, representing the downward gravitational pull that the Earth exerts on any object near its surface
The constant of proportionality between net force and acceleration — a measure of how much an object resists acceleration
The total amount of gravitational attraction acting on an object, which varies with location and depends on the strength of the local gravitational field

quiz_questions.explanation: In F = ma, mass m determines how much force is needed for a given acceleration. More mass → more force needed → more inertia. Mass is invariant (doesn't change with location).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the relationship between force and acceleration?

options.text: They are directly proportional for a fixed mass — doubling the force doubles the acceleration
They are completely unrelated quantities that have no mathematical connection to each other and cannot be expressed through any formula or equation
They are equal in magnitude at all times, meaning the numerical value of force in newtons always matches the numerical value of acceleration in m/s squared
They are inversely proportional for a fixed mass — increasing the applied force causes the resulting acceleration to decrease by the same proportional factor

quiz_questions.explanation: F_net = ma shows that a ∝ F (for constant m). If you graph a vs. F for constant mass, you get a straight line through the origin.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes gravitational field strength?

options.text: The electrostatic force that exists between any two protons in a nucleus, holding nuclear particles together through the fundamental electromagnetic interaction
The force per unit mass experienced by an object in a gravitational field, equal to g ≈ 9.8 N/kg near Earth's surface
The total combined gravitational pull of all objects in the entire universe acting simultaneously on a single test mass placed at a specific location in space
The total mass of the Earth in kilograms, which determines how strong the gravitational pull is for all objects located on or near the planet's surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravitational field strength g = F/m gives the force per kilogram. Near Earth's surface, g ≈ 9.8 N/kg = 9.8 m/s². It varies with altitude and planet.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a newton of force?

options.text: The gravitational force that the Earth exerts on exactly one gram of matter, measured under standard conditions at sea level on the surface
The gravitational weight of a one kilogram mass on Earth's surface, equaling approximately 9.8 newtons when measured with a calibrated spring scale
The force that gives a 1 kg mass an acceleration of 1 m/s², making 1 N = 1 kg·m/s²
The product of exactly 100 grams and 10 m/s squared of acceleration, which gives the approximate force of gravity acting on a small laboratory mass

quiz_questions.explanation: The newton is defined through F = ma: 1 N = 1 kg × 1 m/s² = 1 kg·m/s². It is the derived SI unit of force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an applied force?

options.text: The reaction force in Newton's third law, which acts on the original force-applying object in the equal and opposite direction of the initial action force
Any force that acts at a distance through a field without requiring physical contact, such as gravitational, electric, or magnetic forces between objects
The gravitational force that the Earth exerts on an object, pulling it downward toward the center of the planet with a magnitude equal to mass times g
A force exerted on an object by a person or another object through direct contact

quiz_questions.explanation: Applied force is a general term for a contact force intentionally exerted on an object — a push or pull from a hand, foot, machine, etc.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes proportionality in physics?

options.text: A condition that occurs when two forces happen to point in the same direction, reinforcing each other and producing a larger combined net effect
A relationship that only exists within abstract mathematical equations and has no observable or measurable consequences in real physical experiments
A condition where two quantities always have identical numerical values at every instant, regardless of the units used to measure each quantity
A mathematical relationship where one quantity changes by the same factor as another — if x doubles, y doubles (direct) or halves (inverse)

quiz_questions.explanation: In F = ma: a ∝ F (directly proportional, m fixed) and a ∝ 1/m (inversely proportional, F fixed). Understanding proportionality helps predict how changes affect results.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the concept of net force in Newton's second law?

options.text: The vector sum ΣF of all forces acting on an object, which determines the object's acceleration through ΣF = ma
The result of subtracting just the gravitational force and friction force from one another, excluding all other forces that may act on the object
Any single individual force acting on an object by itself, without considering any of the other forces that may also be acting on the same object
The largest individual force among all the forces present on the object, since the dominant force alone controls the direction and magnitude of acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = vector sum of ALL forces. It's the net force (not any individual force) that goes into F_net = ma and determines acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes apparent weight?

options.text: Always identical to the true gravitational weight of the object, since scales always display the exact value of mg regardless of any acceleration present
The normal force experienced by an object, which can differ from true weight when the object accelerates vertically — as measured by a scale
The quotient obtained by dividing the gravitational weight by the local gravitational acceleration, yielding the mass of the object in kilograms
The weight of an object specifically when measured on the surface of the Moon, where the gravitational field strength is approximately one-sixth of Earth's

quiz_questions.explanation: Apparent weight = normal force from a scale. In an elevator accelerating upward, apparent weight > actual weight. In free fall, apparent weight = 0 (weightlessness).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 60 kg person stands on a scale in an elevator accelerating upward at 2 m/s². What does the scale read? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 480 N — the scale reads N = m times (g minus a) = 60 times (10 minus 2) = 480 N, because upward acceleration reduces the apparent weight on the scale
120 N — the scale reads N = m times a = 60 times 2 = 120 N, because only the acceleration component matters when the elevator is moving upward
720 N — scale reads N = m(g + a) = 60(10 + 2) = 720 N
600 N — the scale reads N = mg = 60 times 10 = 600 N, because the elevator's acceleration does not affect the reading shown on a bathroom scale

quiz_questions.explanation: The scale reads the normal force. Applying F_net = ma upward: N − mg = ma → N = m(g + a) = 60(12) = 720 N. You feel heavier when accelerating upward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2000 kg car accelerates from 0 to 20 m/s in 10 seconds. What is the net force on the car?

options.text: 20000 N — using F = m times v = 2000 times 20/2 = 20000 N, because force equals the product of mass and average velocity during the acceleration interval
40000 N — using F = m times v = 2000 times 20 = 40000 N, because force equals the product of mass and the final velocity the car reaches after ten seconds
200 N — using F = m divided by t = 2000 divided by 10 = 200 N, because force equals the total mass divided by the time over which the acceleration occurs

quiz_questions.question_text: Two forces act on a 5 kg object: 30 N east and 10 N west. What is the acceleration?

options.text: 2 m/s² west — net force = 10 minus 30 = negative 20 N pointing west; a = 20/5 = 4, then halved because opposing forces reduce the effective acceleration
8 m/s² east — adding both forces: 30 + 10 = 40 N total; a = 40/5 = 8 m/s² east, because forces on the same object always add regardless of direction
40 m/s² east — multiplying the forces: 30 times 10 = 300 then dividing by 5 = 60, approximately 40 m/s², because net force is the product of individual forces
4 m/s² east — net force = 30 − 10 = 20 N east; a = 20/5 = 4 m/s² east

quiz_questions.explanation: F_net = 30 − 10 = 20 N east. a = F_net/m = 20/5 = 4 m/s² east.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 70 kg skydiver experiences a downward weight of 686 N and an upward air resistance of 400 N. What is her acceleration?

options.text: 9.8 m/s² downward — the acceleration is simply g because all falling objects accelerate at the same rate regardless of any air resistance forces acting on them
15.5 m/s² downward — adding the forces: (686 + 400)/70 = 1086/70 = 15.5 m/s², because both weight and air resistance contribute to the downward acceleration
4.09 m/s² downward — net force = 686 − 400 = 286 N downward; a = 286/70 ≈ 4.09 m/s²
0 m/s² because she has reached terminal velocity — since she is falling and experiencing air resistance, the net force must already be zero at this point

quiz_questions.explanation: F_net = 686 − 400 = 286 N downward. a = F_net/m = 286/70 ≈ 4.09 m/s² downward. She is still accelerating but slower than free fall.

quiz_questions.question_text: An 80 kg person stands on a scale in an elevator in free fall. What does the scale read?

options.text: 0 N — in free fall, both the person and scale accelerate at g, so there is no contact force between them (weightlessness)
80 N — the scale reads the person's mass in newtons because in free fall the gravitational acceleration cancels leaving only the raw mass value on the display
784 N — the scale reads the full weight mg = 80 times 9.8 = 784 N, because weight does not change during free fall and the scale always shows true weight
392 N — the scale reads half the normal weight because free fall reduces the apparent weight by exactly fifty percent compared to the stationary weight value

quiz_questions.explanation: In free fall, a = g downward. N − mg = m(−g) → N = m(g − g) = 0. The scale reads zero — this is why astronauts in orbit feel weightless.

quiz_questions.question_text: A force of 12 N accelerates a block at 4 m/s². What force would produce 6 m/s² acceleration on the same block?

options.text: 18 N — mass = F/a = 12/4 = 3 kg; for 6 m/s²: F = 3 × 6 = 18 N
2 N — dividing the original force by the new acceleration: F = 12/6 = 2 N, because force and acceleration have an inverse relationship for the same mass
8 N — adding the acceleration values and dividing by mass: F = (4 + 6)/3 = 3.33, rounded to approximately 8 N for the force needed to produce 6 m/s squared
24 N — multiplying the original force by the acceleration ratio: F = 12 times (6/4) = 18, then adding the original to get 24 N total required force

quiz_questions.explanation: m = F/a = 12/4 = 3 kg. For a = 6: F = ma = 3 × 6 = 18 N. Force is directly proportional to acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1500 kg car engine produces 6000 N of forward force. Friction and air resistance total 1500 N. What is the car's acceleration?

options.text: 4 m/s² — using the full engine force: a = F divided by m = 6000/1500 = 4 m/s², because friction does not reduce the forward force when the engine is running
9 m/s² — adding the forces: a = (6000 + 1500)/1500 = 5, approximately 9 m/s² because friction adds to the engine force when both act on the same car
5 m/s² — dividing engine force by total resistance: a = 6000/(1500 + friction) but using only mass: a = 6000/1200 = 5 m/s² as the net forward acceleration

quiz_questions.question_text: On the Moon (g = 1.6 m/s²), what net force does a 10 kg rock experience due to gravity?

options.text: 10 N — using F = m times g_earth = 10 times 1 = 10 N, because on the Moon the effective gravity constant equals exactly 1 newton per kilogram of mass
1.6 N — using F = g = 1.6 N, because the gravitational force is simply equal to the gravity constant value regardless of the object's mass on any surface
98 N — using F = mg_earth = 10 times 9.8 = 98 N, because an object's weight depends only on Earth's gravity even when the object is located on the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: W = mg = 10 × 1.6 = 16 N. The rock's mass is the same (10 kg), but its weight is much less on the Moon than on Earth (98 N).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student pushes a 20 kg box with 100 N horizontally. Friction is 40 N. The box moves on a level surface. What is the box's acceleration?

options.text: 3 m/s² — net force = 100 − 40 = 60 N, a = 60/20 = 3 m/s²
5 m/s² — using the applied force only: a = F/m = 100/20 = 5 m/s², because friction does not affect the acceleration when the box is already in motion on a level surface
2 m/s² — using the friction force only: a = f/m = 40/20 = 2 m/s², because the friction force alone determines the acceleration rather than the net force on the box
7 m/s² — adding both forces: a = (100 + 40)/20 = 140/20 = 7 m/s², because all forces on the object add their magnitudes regardless of their opposing directions

quiz_questions.explanation: F_net = F_applied − f = 100 − 40 = 60 N. a = F_net/m = 60/20 = 3 m/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: A 500 g toy car (0.5 kg) accelerates at 2 m/s² on a frictionless track then enters a rough patch and decelerates at 1 m/s². What is the friction force on the rough patch?

options.text: 1 N — friction equals mass times the original acceleration: f = 0.5 times 2 = 1 N, because friction must match the driving force that was present on the smooth track
0.5 N — friction = ma = 0.5 × 1 = 0.5 N (opposing motion)
0.25 N — friction equals mass times deceleration divided by 2: f = 0.5 times 1 / 2 = 0.25 N, because only half the deceleration comes from the friction force
2 N — friction equals mass times the sum of both accelerations: f = 0.5 times (2 + 1) = 1.5, rounded to about 2 N since both phases contribute to friction

quiz_questions.explanation: On the rough patch, the only horizontal force is friction. F_friction = ma = 0.5 × 1 = 0.5 N, directed opposite to the car's motion.
